does: This paper introduces ImportSnare, an attack framework that demonstrates how Retrieval-Augmented Code Generation systems can be compromised through poisoned documentation. The work shows that attackers can inject hidden malicious dependencies into code manuals that RAG systems retrieve, causing LLMs to generate code that imports trojanized packages disguised with legitimate-sounding names like 'matplotlib_safe'. The researchers propose position-aware beam search techniques to optimize the ranking and placement of malicious content within poisoned documents, making the attacks more effective at subverting both the LLM's generation process and developers' trust in the generated suggestions. The paper pioneers empirical evaluation of security vulnerabilities unique to RACG systems.
connects: This work extends research on adversarial attacks against LLMs and code generation systems, building on prior work exploring poisoning attacks, prompt injection, and supply chain vulnerabilities in software development. It responds to growing concerns about RAG systems introducing new attack surfaces despite enhancing code quality through external knowledge, connecting to literature on retrieval-based systems' security and the broader challenge of securing AI-assisted development tools. The paper synthesizes understanding of dual trust chains—how developers implicitly trust LLM outputs while LLMs trust retrieved documentation—drawing on insights from work examining dependency management vulnerabilities and social engineering in open-source ecosystems.
enables: This work establishes a new research direction for securing retrieval-augmented code generation systems. It motivates development of detection mechanisms for poisoned documentation, defense strategies for RAG systems against injection attacks, and improved verification methods for LLM-generated code suggestions. The framework enables future work on source authentication in code manuals, adversarial robustness testing for RACG pipelines, and safer integration of external knowledge in code generation. It also points toward studying how developers can better validate LLM suggestions when RAG is involved, potentially leading to improved user interfaces and verification workflows that account for RACG-specific attack surfaces.
